era_context: Mid-17th century: European powers consolidating colonial empires; the Qing Dynasty under Kangxi Emperor consolidating control over China. The Three Feudatories Rebellion (1673–1681) ongoing in southern China. Louis XIV's France at the height of its power; the Ottoman Empire in gradual decline. The Scientific Revolution advancing in Europe.

events.title: Treaty of Westminster ends Third Anglo-Dutch War
events.region: England, Dutch Republic, and colonial territories
events.description: England and the Dutch Republic concluded the Third Anglo-Dutch War with the Treaty of Westminster in February 1674. The treaty confirmed English control of New York (formerly New Amsterdam) and other colonial territories, marking a significant shift in the balance of colonial power in North America. The Dutch ceded their North American colonies to England, effectively ending Dutch colonial ambitions in the region. This treaty solidified England's position as the dominant colonial power in North America.

sources.name: Treaty of Westminster (1674), primary diplomatic document

events.certainty_note: The treaty date and terms are well-established in diplomatic records.

events.title: Three Feudatories Rebellion continues in Qing China
events.description: The Three Feudatories Rebellion, which began in 1673, continued to challenge Qing central authority in southern China. By 1674, the rebellion was in full military swing, with Wu Sangui and other rebel leaders controlling significant territories in the south. The Kangxi Emperor's forces were engaged in sustained military campaigns to suppress the rebellion. The conflict would continue until 1681, representing one of the most serious challenges to early Qing rule.

sources.name: Qing imperial records and military archives

events.certainty_note: The rebellion's progression is well-documented in Qing records.

events.title: Shivaji crowned as Maratha king
events.description: Shivaji Bhonsle, the Maratha leader, was formally crowned as king (Chhatrapati) in 1674, establishing the Maratha Empire as an independent political entity. This coronation ceremony, held at Raigad Fort, marked the formal declaration of Maratha independence from Mughal overlordship. Shivaji's establishment of the Maratha state represented a significant challenge to Mughal dominance in India and laid the foundation for the Maratha Confederacy's later expansion. The coronation was a symbolic and political milestone in Indian history.

sources.name: Marathi chronicles and later compilations of Maratha history

events.certainty_note: Shivaji's coronation in 1674 is well-documented in Maratha sources and historiographic consensus, though the exact date (May 1674) is sometimes debated.

events.title: Louis XIV's court moves to Versailles
events.description: King Louis XIV of France began the process of moving the royal court from Paris to the newly constructed Palace of Versailles. While construction had begun earlier, 1674 marked a significant phase in the establishment of Versailles as the primary royal residence. The move symbolized Louis XIV's absolute power and his desire to control the French nobility by concentrating them at court. Versailles became the model for absolute monarchy in Europe and a symbol of French cultural dominance.

sources.name: Historiographic consensus on Louis XIV and Versailles

events.certainty_note: The move to Versailles was gradual; 1674 marks a significant phase but not a single event. The exact date of the 'move' is somewhat arbitrary.

events.title: Newton's optical experiments and corpuscular theory of light
events.description: Isaac Newton continued his optical experiments and developed his corpuscular (particle) theory of light, which challenged the prevailing wave theory. Newton's work on light and color, conducted during his annus mirabilis (1665–1666) and refined in subsequent years, was being formalized and prepared for publication. His optical work would be published in the Opticks (1704), but the theoretical foundations were being solidified in the 1670s.

sources.name: Historiographic consensus on Newton's optical work

events.certainty_note: Newton's optical work was conducted over many years; 1674 is approximate for ongoing refinement of his theories.

disconfirming_evidence: The Treaty of Westminster is sometimes portrayed as a clear English victory, but modern scholarship emphasizes that the Dutch retained significant commercial advantages and that the treaty reflected a compromise rather than a decisive English triumph. The move to Versailles is often dated to 1682, when Louis XIV formally moved the court; the 1674 date reflects the beginning of the process rather than a single decisive event. Shivaji's coronation is sometimes portrayed as a complete break from Mughal authority, but modern scholarship recognizes that Maratha-Mughal relations remained complex and that Shivaji continued to negotiate with the Mughal Empire even after his coronation.
historiographic_note: Documentation for 1674 is rich in European sources, particularly French and English diplomatic records. Indian sources on Shivaji and the Maratha Confederacy are abundant but less frequently synthesized in English-language historiography. Chinese sources on the Three Feudatories Rebellion are well-documented. Scientific work by Newton is well-documented in his manuscripts and correspondence. African, Southeast Asian, and other non-European events are underrepresented in available sources.

graph_edges.note: Treaty of Westminster established English dominance in North American colonies
Shivaji's coronation established the Maratha Empire and led to later expansion
Versailles became the symbol of absolute monarchy in Europe
